note: Kept per-scene, as confirmed — each scene generates its own clip independently; stitching all scene clips into one finished commercial happens externally, outside this tool. The source doc describes a single whole-project 'Final Production Render' assembling every approved stage at once — adapted below to per-scene scope: 'this scene's Blueprint/Character/Prop/Background Sheets + Music Brief' instead of the whole project's. Cross-scene concerns (adding new scenes, transitions between scenes) trimmed out since they don't apply to a single clip.

universalStyle.label: Universal Production Bible Style
universalStyle.text:
Maintain one consistent visual identity.

• Apple keynote presentation quality
• Cannes Lions commercial quality
• Netflix production consistency
• Weta Workshop attention to detail

The final video must feel like it was directed by an award-winning commercial director and produced by a premium production house.

movie.label: Final Movie Clip
movie.note: content.geminiInstruction removed — animation direction is now a plain designer-set field, no Gemini drafting. Transition/B-roll moved here from Stage 4 — they're video-editing concepts (how this clip cuts into the next one, what supplementary footage to cut in) that a still keyframe image can't represent, so they belong to the actual clip, not the Scene Blueprint.

fieldsSchema.label: Animation direction
presets: Slow push-in on the subject, hold at the end.
Static shot with a gentle handheld sway.
Pan left to right, following the product into frame.
Slow pull back to reveal the full scene.
Quick zoom in for emphasis, then hold steady.

optionHelp.None: What: no supplementary footage. When to use: the main shot alone tells the story. Example: a simple testimonial clip, no product insert needed.
optionHelp.Product Macro Shot: What: an extreme close-up insert of the product. When to use: to highlight fine detail like label or texture. Example: a close-up of a jar's lid twisting open.
optionHelp.Product Spin: What: a rotating, 360°-style shot of the product. When to use: to show its full form and branding. Example: a bottle slowly rotating to reveal its label.
optionHelp.Slow-Motion Detail: What: a slowed-down insert emphasizing a small action. When to use: for a satisfying, premium-feel moment. Example: cream dripping slowly off a spoon.
optionHelp.Texture Close-up: What: a tight shot on surface texture. When to use: to emphasize quality or ingredients. Example: a close-up on the weave of a fabric.
optionHelp.Lifestyle Cutaway: What: a brief shot of the product in real use. When to use: to show it fitting into daily life. Example: someone casually sipping the drink at their desk.

optionHelp.Hard Cut: What: an instant cut with no effect. When to use: the default choice for most edits, keeps pacing snappy. Example: cutting straight from the product shot to the customer's reaction.
optionHelp.Whip Pan: What: a fast, blurred camera swipe between shots. When to use: for energetic, fast-paced transitions. Example: whipping from the kitchen to the dining table.
optionHelp.Match Cut: What: a cut where the next shot's composition or action matches the previous one. When to use: for a smooth, clever visual continuity. Example: a jar lid closing cuts to a door closing in the next scene.
optionHelp.Fade: What: the shot gradually fades to/from black or the next shot. When to use: for a soft, emotional, or scene-ending transition. Example: fading out after the final call-to-action line.
optionHelp.Zoom Transition: What: a quick zoom into or out of frame to transition. When to use: for punchy, modern social-style edits. Example: zooming into the logo before cutting to the next scene.

outputInstructions.label: Master Final Production Render Prompt (per scene)
outputInstructions.text:
Create this scene's final commercial-quality clip exactly from its approved Scene Blueprint.

OBJECTIVE

Execute this scene's approved Scene Blueprint without inventing new story elements.
Maintain complete continuity with the Character Sheets, Prop Sheets, Background Sheet, and Music Brief referenced for this scene.

FOLLOW EXACTLY

• This scene's Blueprint
• The Character Sheets featured in this scene
• The Prop Sheets featured in this scene
• This scene's Background Sheet
• The Music Brief

CONTINUITY

• Character identity
• Hair & wardrobe
• Props
• Backgrounds
• Time of day
• Weather
• Lighting
• Camera direction
• Screen direction
• Dialogue timing
• Emotion progression
• Branding

CINEMATOGRAPHY

• Hollywood commercial quality
• Premium advertising cinematography
• Natural human motion
• Realistic facial animation
• Accurate lip sync (if dialogue exists)
• Correct eye-line
• Natural hand interactions
• Realistic physics
• Smooth camera movement
• Consistent color grading
• Feature-film lighting
• Premium depth of field

EDITING

• Respect the planned shot duration
• Honor the specified transition into/out of this clip
• Include the specified B-roll insert, if any
• Preserve pacing
• Match music cues
• Match sound effects
• Broadcast-ready finish

DO NOT

• Change story
• Add characters
• Change wardrobe
• Change props
• Redesign locations
• Change branding

DIMENSIONS

Render at the highest resolution Flow supports (minimum 1080p), in the aspect ratio matching the target platform — vertical 9:16 for Reels/Shorts/TikTok, 16:9 for YouTube/landscape.

OUTPUT

Generate ONE polished, commercial-quality video clip for this scene, faithful to its approved Scene Blueprint and the Production Bible.

final_video.label: Final Video
final_video.note: One per project, auto-created when Stage 4 locks (alongside the per-scene Movie pairing), never manually deletable. Upload-only — designer stitches every scene's approved clip together externally and uploads the one finished result here. This is the only thing the customer's public download for Stage 5 actually serves (see pay.ts) — the per-scene clips stay a production/designer artifact, not something the customer needs to see.
